text: The surgeon operates with a cautery scissor in the right hand, a fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, and a prograsp instrument in the fourth robotic arm.
The patient is positioned in Trendelenburg, with the camera port inserted at the umbilicus, providing a view of the surgical field for the robotic prostatectomy.
An eight millimeter air seal assistant port is visible, providing insufflation for the procedure. The assistant uses this port to pass needles and place the specimen bag.
The surgeon has released the bowel, indicating the initial steps of the transperitoneal prostatectomy procedure.

The surgeon performs a posterior dissection of the seminal vesicles, utilizing the cautery scissor in the right hand and fenestrated Maryland bipolar in the left hand, with the prograsp in the fourth arm.
The surgeon makes an initial incision approximately one centimeter above the line between the whitish peritoneal reflection on the bladder and the yellowish perirectal fat.
After completing the medial dissection, the surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to maneuver into the contralateral shoulder area, providing a view of the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the seminal vesicles, ensuring that no residual tissue remains attached, leaving the seminal vesicles clearly exposed.
The surgeon uses both monopolar and bipolar electrocautery devices set at twenty-five to minimize thermal spread and reduce the risk of nerve injury during the prostatectomy.
The surgeon performs a posterior dissection to expose the seminal vesicles (SVs), ensuring clear visualization of these structures without any surrounding tissue.
The surgeon begins by exposing the vas deferens, carefully revealing its structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm of the robotic system to grasp the vas deferens and lift it straight up, providing clear exposure for further dissection.
The fourth robotic arm actively provides exposure during the operation, assisting in maintaining clear visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon locates the tip of the seminal vesicle (SV) and adjusts the camera to a second position, providing a view down into the contralateral shoulder to reveal the lateral aspect of the SV.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the seminal vesicle, maintaining close proximity to its surface to ensure precise separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon cuts the vas deferens in line with the lateral aspect of the seminal vesicle, allowing both ends to retract and remain out of the surgical field.
The surgeon makes an incision on the right side, adjacent to the ligament, and continues the dissection until the underlying bone is exposed.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection along a natural plane, having made incisions on both sides to expose the pubic bone.

The surgeon connects the dissection points, ensuring alignment by visualizing the pubic bone. The view alternates between the left and right sides to confirm the correct pathway, avoiding an anterior approach that could lead into the rectus muscle.
The surgeon uses electrocautery set at forty-forty to clean up adipose tissue on the prostate, preparing to address the lymph nodes.

The surgeon adjusts the cautery settings to forty-forty to facilitate the dropping of the bladder and proceeds with the dissection of the lymph nodes, optimizing the procedure by minimizing instrument changes.
The surgeon utilizes the previously developed plane between the perivascular fat and the nodes to clearly define the location of the node packet, facilitating precise dissection.

The obturator nerve becomes visible, emerging prominently in the surgical field. The surgeon carefully positions instruments to hug the adjacent vein, aiming to remove surrounding tissue while preserving the nerve.
The surgeon places a clip on the lymphatic vessels returning from the leg, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses cautery to seal around the node packet, ensuring complete coverage regardless of visible lymphatics.
The surgeon seals tissue around the vein, including areas on top of the vein, from behind the vein, and between the pelvic side wall muscle and the vein.

The surgeon clips the upper and lower sides of the vein and uses bipolar cautery to seal the tissue in between. The surgical field is inspected to ensure no lymphatics are left behind during the dissection on the underside of the vein.
The surgeon transitions to the opposite side to perform a bladder neck dissection, continuing the procedure symmetrically.
The surgeon adjusts the cautery setting to twenty-five to minimize tissue charring and proceeds to make an incision over the prostate.
The video shows the initial incision made in the bladder neck, positioned on top of the prostate, avoiding direct entry into the prostate tissue.
The surgeon makes an incision and uses the left hand to apply constant backwards pressure on the bladder neck, effectively peeling the prostate and bladder apart.
The surgeon gently touches the tissue with the cautery device, avoiding deep penetration into the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to grasp the urethra and lifts it towards the anterior abdominal wall, revealing the posterior bladder neck for further dissection.

The surgeon initiates an incision at the corners of the bladder neck, avoiding the midline six o'clock position. The corners are carefully released to maintain tension on the bladder neck, ensuring precise separation from the prostate.
The surgeon demonstrates the fixed size of the bladder neck after releasing the corners and completing the incision at the six o'clock position.
The surgeon uses a low cautery setting to carefully separate the prostate from the bladder neck, minimizing tissue char and ensuring precise dissection.
The surgeon stretches the bladder neck fibers with the left hand, clearly exposing them while ensuring the dissection remains away from the prostate.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to dissect through the bladder muscle at the corners on both sides of the prostate, revealing underlying fat tissue with no remaining bladder muscle fibers visible on the lateral aspect.
The surgeon removes muscle from the left and right sides at the base of the prostate, ensuring the bladder is not tethered and is positioned lower in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs rectal dissection, with the rectum clearly visible in the surgical field, ensuring it is not obstructing the view.

The surgeon makes an incision in the posterior Denonvilliers' fascia, entering the perirectal fat plane. The perirectal fat is carefully peeled off the posterior aspect of the prostate. The high anterior release of the prostatic fascia, also known as the veil of Aphrodite, has been completed.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the prostate to identify the neurovascular bundle, utilizing the air seal system to maintain constant pneumo pressure, which minimizes venous bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon uses a robotic clip applier to secure the pedicle near the prostate, placing the clip high to avoid injury to the neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to demonstrate the proximity of the pedicle to the neurovascular bundle at the six o'clock position on the screen. The neurovascular bundle is observed turning and moving posterolateral to the prostate, where it has been peeled away following a previous incision.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the non-VAs on the posterior aspect of the prostate gland and proceeds to cut this section, ensuring precise removal of the tissue.
The surgical field is maintained at a pneumo pressure of six using the air seal system, effectively minimizing venous bleeding despite continuous suction to remove smoke from the operative area.
The surgeon performs a high anterior release on the prostatic fascia, carefully maneuvering the pedicle to the side to visualize the prostate's turning point and locate its bottom edge.
The surgeon positions the robotic clip applier to take the entire pedicle in one clip, aiming to maximize nerve sparing by maintaining the integrity of the neurovascular bundle on the downside.

The surgeon is positioned at the prostate capsule, with the Denonvilliers' fascia edge visible medially. The neurovascular bundle is being carefully dissected off, extending the nerve-sparing dissection to the apex of the prostate gland and beyond.

The surgeon cuts through the dorsal venous complex (DVC) after ensuring the neurovascular bundle is released on both sides, minimizing the risk of accidental damage. The DVC is then over-sewn to control bleeding.

The surgeon operates close to the prostate, maintaining pneumo pressure at six, with minimal bleeding observed from the dorsal venous complex (DVC). The urethra is visible, and the surgeon cuts through it with the catheter in place.

The surgeon pulls the catheter back to expose the posterior aspect of the urethra, aiming to maximize urethral length during the prostatectomy. The procedure approaches completion as the posterior urethra is visualized.

The surgeon performs an instrument change to needle drivers. The prostatectomy specimen is shown, highlighting successful nerve-sparing techniques. The focus is on the capsule of the prostate posterolaterally and the base of the prostate adjacent to the seminal vesicles where the pedicle was located.
The surgical field shows minimal residual tissue at the site where the pedicle was taken close to the prostate, indicating successful preservation of the neurovascular bundles in that area.

The surgeon applies a figure-of-eight stitch to the dorsal venous complex (DVC) using an ovicryl suture and a CT-I needle. The stitch is placed through the pubic symphysis to secure the DVC, with additional throws if necessary.

The surgeon compresses the dorsal venous complex (DVC) against the underside of the pubic bone to prevent postoperative bleeding. An ovicryl suture on a CT-1 needle is used for the Rocco stitch, horizontally placed under the urethra to incorporate the posterior plate behind it. The cut edge of the non-bladder structures and the peritoneal edge near the seminal vesicles are also sutured.

The surgeon positions the posterior bladder away from the bladder neck to prevent interference with the anastomosis. A slider knot is used to secure the tissues together, aiming to prevent postoperative bleeding that could disrupt the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a Stratafix barbed suture to perform the anastomosis, starting at the four or five o'clock position. Two stitches are placed through the urethra and three through the bladder, cinching them down to complete the posterior aspect of the anastomosis. The mucosa is carefully aligned to prevent leaks and avoid the need for a drain.

The surgeon completes the anastomosis, ensuring no gaps are left for urine leakage. A catheter is inserted, and the bladder is filled with saline to check for leaks. The absence of leakage confirms the integrity of the anastomosis.
